critic_raw:
The design explicitly acknowledges that 'cross-sectional predictive signals are primary, assuming that any temporal autocorrelation effects introduced by hospital practices are weak' to justify a stratified random split. This assumption is not tested and is unlikely to hold. Hospital readmission is driven by discharge decisions, post-discharge care plans, and follow-up visit timing — all of which are temporally structured by institutional practices (discharge criteria, weekend vs. weekday discharge, staffing patterns) and by patient health trajectories that unfold over days to weeks. The assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation is not merely a modeling simplification; it is a testable empirical claim that the design never tests. The simplest test — comparing performance of a temporal split vs. random split — is absent.

Second, the RNN is trained on 'sequences of patient visits' but the design describes only 10,000 patient visits over three years, not 10,000 patients. If a patient has an average of 3-5 visits, the effective patient count is 2,000-3,000, with an even smaller number of readmission events (typically 10-20% base rate for 30-day readmission). An RNN with meaningful sequence depth requires enough patients with multi-visit histories; with ~2,000-3,000 patients, an RNN may not have sufficient data to learn meaningful sequential dependencies, and logistic regression — which makes efficient use of limited data — would be expected to match or outperform it on sample-size grounds alone. The design does not address this sample size adequacy concern.

Third, the comparison is between an RNN on visit sequences and logistic regression on demographic and clinical features. These models access different feature sets: the RNN sees the temporal structure of visits, while logistic regression sees aggregated demographics and clinical summaries. If logistic regression had access to the same feature aggregates that the RNN computes implicitly, the comparison would test architecture rather than feature set. As designed, a superior RNN could indicate sequence modeling benefits, or simply that visit sequence counts and temporal patterns are predictive features not available to the logistic regression. The design conflates these.

Fourth, the design specifies stratifying splits by hospital, which is appropriate for confound control. But in a three-year, 10,000-visit dataset spanning multiple hospitals, some hospitals may have only a few hundred visits — too few to estimate readmission rates reliably. The stratification by hospital combined with the small dataset size may produce hospital-level test subsets too small for stable subgroup estimates. The design does not report minimum subgroup sizes or acknowledge this statistical fragility.
all_issues_raised: The assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmission data is an untested empirical claim; the simplest test (comparing random vs. temporal split performance) is absent, and institutional practices like weekend discharge and staffing patterns create detectable temporal structure.

With only 10,000 patient visits across 3 years and ~2,000-3,000 implied patients, the RNN lacks sufficient multi-visit sequences to learn meaningful temporal dependencies — logistic regression is expected to match or outperform under this sample regime, undermining the experimental contrast.

The comparison conflates model architecture with feature access: the RNN uses visit sequences while logistic regression uses aggregated demographics; a logistic regression with sequence-derived aggregates would isolate whether architecture or feature representation drives any observed difference.

Hospital-level stratification combined with small overall dataset size produces hospital subsets likely too small for stable subgroup performance estimates, and the design does not specify minimum subgroup sizes or acknowledge this statistical fragility.
all_issues_adjudicated: The assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmission data is an untested empirical claim; the simplest test (comparing random vs. temporal split performance) is absent, and institutional practices like weekend discharge and staffing patterns create detectable temporal structure.

With only 10,000 patient visits across 3 years and ~2,000-3,000 implied patients, the RNN lacks sufficient multi-visit sequences to learn meaningful temporal dependencies — logistic regression is expected to match or outperform under this sample regime, undermining the experimental contrast.

The comparison conflates model architecture with feature access: the RNN uses visit sequences while logistic regression uses aggregated demographics; a logistic regression with sequence-derived aggregates would isolate whether architecture or feature representation drives any observed difference.

Hospital-level stratification combined with small overall dataset size produces hospital subsets likely too small for stable subgroup performance estimates, and the design does not specify minimum subgroup sizes or acknowledge this statistical fragility.
